narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school and then hearing your application won't move forward because our model sees a 62% chance you'll struggle to pass the bar exam feels like a gut punch, especially when you've poured so much into getting here. Picture the model looking at your story like a coach scouting a player: it weighs what predicts success on the field most heavily, and for you, your Year 3 grades landing in the bottom decile stood out as the biggest signal of trouble ahead, more than anything else. That early dip in Year 1, also in the lowest decile, reinforced it, painting a picture of consistent academic hurdles in a tough law program. Your LSAT score of 27.5 added to that concern, sitting lower than what typically powers through the bar's demands. Coming from a low family income bracket tugged in the same direction, as the model knows that can mean fewer resources for the long haul of studying and prep. Your background as a Native American male, while full of strength and resilience, aligned with patterns where others in similar spots have faced steeper odds. Even your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.5 and full-time commitment couldn't fully counterbalance those core worries—they pulled toward success but weren't enough against the heavier hitters. It's like the model saw a talented athlete with heart but patterns suggesting the bar's marathon might prove too grueling. This isn't about your worth or potential; it's a data-driven snapshot urging us to set everyone up for real wins. If you boost those later grades or retake the LSAT, your story could shift dramatically—many have turned it around from here. Hang in there; you've got the drive, and this is just one chapter.
